statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, find the number of reverse pairs in the array. A reverse pair is a pair of elements at positions i and j such that i < j and arr[i] > 2 * arr[j]. The task is to write a function that takes an array of integers as input and returns the number of reverse pairs in the array. The function should be efficient and should be able to handle large inputs. The input array will contain only integers. The function should return the count of reverse pairs as an integer. The input array will be given on the standard input and the output should be printed on the standard output.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer n, which is the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n integers, which are the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a single integer, which is the number of reverse pairs in the array.
statement.notes: The input array will contain only integers. The function should be efficient and should be able to handle large inputs.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: There are no reverse pairs in the array

hints.text: To solve this problem, you can use a modified merge sort algorithm. The idea is to count the number of reverse pairs in the array while sorting it.

When merging two sorted subarrays, you need to count the number of elements in the second subarray that are smaller than the current element in the first subarray. This is because these elements form a reverse pair with the current element.

You can achieve this by using a temporary array to store the elements from the second subarray that are smaller than the current element in the first subarray. Then, you can count the number of elements in this temporary array.

However, this approach has a time complexity of O(n^2) due to the counting of reverse pairs in each merge step. To improve this, you can use a technique called 'binary indexed tree' (Fenwick tree) to count the number of elements smaller than the current element in the first subarray.

By using a Fenwick tree, you can count the number of elements smaller than the current element in O(log n) time, resulting in a total time complexity of O(n log n) for the modified merge sort algorithm.

editorial.title: Reverse Pairs Problem Analysis
editorial.intuition: The brute force approach is correct but slow because it checks every possible pair of elements in the array, resulting in a time complexity of O(n^2). This is due to the nested loops that compare each element with every other element that comes after it.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution works by iterating over the array and for each element, it checks all the elements that come after it to see if the current element is greater than twice the value of the later element. If so, it increments the count of reverse pairs.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach would involve using a modified merge sort or a binary indexed tree to reduce the time complexity.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution would utilize a data structure that allows for efficient range queries, such as a balanced binary search tree or a Fenwick tree, to count the number of elements that are less than half of the current element in O(n log n) time complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal approach can be proven by showing that it correctly counts all pairs of elements where the first element is greater than twice the second element, which is the definition of a reverse pair. This is achieved by ensuring that the data structure used can accurately report the number of elements that satisfy the condition for each element in the array.
editorial.dryRun: To validate the solution, a dry run can be performed with a small sample input to manually verify that the output matches the expected result.
commonMistakes: Not considering all pairs of elements
Incorrectly counting pairs where the first element is not greater than twice the second
Failing to handle edge cases such as an empty array or an array with a single element
Using an inefficient data structure that does not support range queries
Not properly updating the count of reverse pairs
